What's going everyone? Welcome to the three phase hybrid conversion process. So this is kind of the overarching picture of the conversion process that we go from zero to hero, from brick and mortar to full hybrid. The last of three phases is entirely optional, but the first two is kind of the sequence and the objectives that we're trying to accomplish. So phase one is called traffic acquisition. So the whole point here is to sell people from paid advertisements, right? The first goal for you should be to sell 30 per month at 599, so that brings in $18,000 a month of coming in from online revenue. Your second goal should be to get the 15 online recurring clients at $77 a week. So that's 15,000 a month in recurring revenue from your hybrid clients. Once you've accomplished that, then you can move on to phase two. So basically do not pass phase one and go to phase two until you have accomplished those two things. So you wanna make sure that you can sell at least 30 people a month on the front end and that you have gotten to 50 online clients. If for some reason you're not able to achieve that, especially if you're doing goal one and you can't get to goal two, then you need to double check on your fulfillment to make sure that your turn is, that you're doing a good job and you're fulfilling your promises so that you can be a good human being and actually help people. Now, once you've passed phase one, and mind you, phase one doesn't stop, you continue to sell to cool traffic always, but then it's just the question of like, okay, now I'm making real money from this thing, what do I do with my gym? And so with your gym at this point, you would enter phase two. So you would then be discussing and showcasing the results that you've been achieving with your hybrid clients to your in-person clientele. And a lot of times the results you're getting with your hybrid clients are gonna be superior because they're personalized, it's farm nutrition based and it's one on one, right? And so the goal here is to take 35% minimum to take the additional $49 a week. Now, 30 and a 5% is like the actual, like what I kind of expect to average, you should shoot for 100%, right? And you should meet with every single person, that's why I say, but shoot for 100%. But if you hit 35%, 40%, that is where you should be sitting at. If you have below that, then something's very wrong, right? Now, if you have 150 EFTs and you do 35%, then you can get another 50 people at $200 a week, $200 a month, excuse me, it's $10,000 a month of added revenue. It's significant, you're not really adding any over it. So this is the fastest way for us to massively increase the profit of the facility in for you, obviously pay for ourselves, and just make sure that we can set you up for the long haul, right? The third phase is phase three, and this is entirely optional, and I just wanna make sure that that's clear. Once your hybrid income exceeds your gym income, okay? You can choose to convert your facility to an entirely hybrid facility. Now that is entirely up to you. So if for some reason you don't, if you're like, I love my gym, I love my community, then just keep it, you know what I mean? Like there's more to life than just making money. If you are purely looking at it as a business, and you're like, I wanna be able to have more time freedom, more geographic freedom, be able to travel when I want to, and not have to be tied in the facility, or in the area, whatever, then this may be an option for you, okay? That being said, you need to make sure that you've passed phase one and phase two, and that you have comfortably established a good amount of revenue, so that if you lose half the customers that you have existing at your gym, that you can still, even once you cut all the overhead associated with fulfilling those customers, that you can still obviously pay yourself, have a healthy amount of profit left over. Now if you've done phase one and phase two correctly, that should be not even a problem, all right? That's more or less it. The only reason you would ever do a flip three before phase one and two is if it was a major extenuating circumstance, which is probably not you, okay? So phase one, then phase two, period, that's it. Make sure that you have more revenue that's coming in from your recurring membership base that you could cover your rent and your costs and paying yourself before you make the flip if you ever choose to do that, okay? Now, the next question is, why do we do this? So the reasoning is that just given our experience, having done this, this seems to be the best approach because human beings are not mathematical, all right? And so a lot of times making a switch when you have a good amount of profit in the bank account and you have this massive recurring revenue that's building every month, that's very profitable, that's growing, you are a lot more confident to make that change and also to sell with conviction to your existing customers that this is a better solution. It's hard to say that until you've experienced it. And so this way, it allows you to only sell the customers that you have once you've already proven to yourself that you can get results with cold traffic clients that have no expectations, right? And the biggest one here is expectations. The reason that it's easy to sell cold traffic into this, it's a hybrid model is because they have no set expectations. And so following the script, they will go along the Epiphany Bridge to understand why this is a superior option, right? Whereas people who have been sold one expectation and then have to basically break that belief and then sell them another, one is more difficult, right? And so there's a certain amount of people who will create noise in your life whenever you change things, promise you, that's just part of business. But especially if we're starting out on our first foot together, what I wanna do is make you how much money first, not deal with how much drama. So that is why phase one and phase two are done in that sequence and then phase three is only after that, if you so desire to do that, okay? I think I covered this one. And yeah, this one just comes from a risk standpoint, is that we had guys when we were in the betas just do a straight flip. They basically sold like 30 people and they were like, holy shit, this is so much better. And then just immediately went to flip. And I think after speaking with all of those, all the guys that did the beta first, they're like, you know, and if I could do it over again, I would have waited a little longer, not once the flip process starts, once the process starts, you gotta go fast. But waiting to stack up more recurring revenue from the online clients, so that they had a bigger cushion making the flip. They're like, it got me really thin making the flip. Now they're super happy about it, et cetera. But like during that period of time, they're like, I would have been a lot less stressed. And so this is a way for you to decrease the risk. And so by doing it this way, there's way less emotional drain on you. There's way less financial risk that you're exposed to. And so it makes it a very easy process of just selling to cold traffic first, make sure you have a nice 15, 20,000 a month in recurring revenue. That's very, very profitable. Then you can bolt on revenue on top of the clients that you already have, which makes your existing business even more profitable too. And then if everything is hunky door, and then only then if you want to go for the freedom play, then you can do that and accept the noise that's gonna happen as a result. So some pros and cons of having the existing facility is that, pros of having the existing facility is that the place from bolt on, and from phase two and three, you're gonna get more from them, because you have an existing traffic that you can add revenue just immediately at no cost. So that's kind of the biggest pro. I mean, it's basically the closest thing to free money. The con is that with those clients come existing expectations, and also if you knew you were gonna do the hybrid from the beginning, you probably would have had a smaller facility, you probably would have structured differently, you probably wouldn't have bought some equipment you did, et cetera. And so don't lament, there's a million things that would have done differently in my life if I knew what I know now, but I didn't. And so that's just part of life, you gotta learn. And so pros is that you have customers that are there, cons is that you might have done structure the business differently. And some people just he and haul when things change. Okay, now pros and cons the first time through, if this is your, like if you are starting your fitness business, then that's amazing. And then you're like gonna literally start on the best foot ever. And I'm sure a lot of the gym lords who are in this community hate you for that. On the flip side, you don't have an existing customer base who you can lean on and upsell immediately to. So that's kind of the downside, but you can start with massively lower overhead and just basically just build a purely profit model with very low liability and risk, which is exactly what you wanna do. So that should conclude the conversion process. I just like to give you the reasoning behind why we do it this way. We do it this way because we've done it before and humans are humans. And this allows you to have the easiest conversion process from where you are and where you're trying to go, minimizing risk, maximizing upside, and just making the process as seamless as possible. So that is the process we're going through. You'll see the videos, phase one, phase two, phase three. After this, just remember, if you're going the Yellowbrook Road, then you're really only watching phase one because it's the only thing you need to focus on for right now. Don't distract yourself. Just focus on getting to those two goals of selling 30 people a month and then building up your recurring to 50 a month minimum before you move on to phase two. All right, rock and roll and keep being great.
